tech-ai.title: OpenAI is bringing on some big guns in the lead-up to its IPO
tech-ai.summary: OpenAI, the company behind ChatGPT, is preparing to go public (an IPO means selling shares so anyone can own a piece of the company). They just hired Noam Shazeer, who co-invented the Transformer technology that powers all modern AI, from Google DeepMind, plus Dean Ball, a former Trump administration AI policy official. This is huge because it shows OpenAI is serious about becoming a major public company and wants top AI talent on board. When companies go public, they need experienced leaders to help run things smoothly. This move signals that OpenAI is confident about its future and is preparing for massive growth and responsibility.

tech-ai.title: Amazon hopes to challenge Nvidia more directly by selling its AI chips
tech-ai.summary: Amazon Web Services (AWS), which is Amazon's cloud computing division (basically their powerful computer servers that businesses rent), wants to sell its own AI chips to other companies and data centers. Right now, Nvidia dominates this market with their expensive GPUs (graphics processing units—specialized computer chips that are really good at AI tasks). Amazon CEO Andy Jassy sees a $50 billion opportunity here. This matters because Amazon selling cheaper alternatives could make AI development more affordable for everyone, including small startups and students. If Amazon succeeds, it could shake up the entire AI industry and give companies more choices instead of relying only on Nvidia.

tech-ai.title: AI inference startup Baseten reportedly raising $1.5B months after its last mega-round
tech-ai.summary: Baseten, a startup that helps companies run AI models quickly and cheaply, is raising $1.5 billion in funding at a $13 billion valuation. 'Inference' is the technical term for when an AI actually does its job—like when ChatGPT answers your question. This funding round shows massive investor confidence in AI infrastructure companies. Basically, as AI becomes more popular, companies need better tools to actually use these AI systems efficiently without spending millions on electricity and servers. Baseten is raising money because investors believe this problem is worth solving and will be hugely profitable. This reflects the current 'inference gold rush'—everyone racing to profit from making AI faster and cheaper.

tech-ai.title: Rivian owners file lawsuit alleging false promises on self-driving features
tech-ai.summary: Rivian, an electric car company founded in 2009, promised owners that their R1 vehicles (the first generation of their cars) would get hands-free driving features eventually. A class-action lawsuit (where multiple people sue together for the same reason) says Rivian lied about when or if this would happen. This matters because self-driving cars are a huge part of the autonomous vehicle industry's future, but many companies overpromise features they can't actually deliver. If Rivian loses this lawsuit, it could set a precedent making companies more honest about what their vehicles can do. It's a reminder that technology companies must be truthful about their capabilities, not just hype what's theoretically possible.

tech-ai.title: Elastic agrees to buy CRV-backed DeductiveAI for up to $85M
tech-ai.summary: Elastic, a company that makes popular software for searching and analyzing data, is buying DeductiveAI, a three-year-old startup that uses AI to find and fix bugs in software code. DeductiveAI is getting up to $85 million for this deal. This matters because finding bugs (mistakes in code) is tedious and expensive—programmers usually have to do it manually. An AI that automates this saves companies tons of money and makes software better. This acquisition shows that AI-powered developer tools are becoming serious business. As AI gets better at understanding code, more companies will want these tools, making software development faster and less error-prone for everyone.

tech-ai.title: Snap spins off AI video team into new company, Dotmo, due to costs
tech-ai.summary: Snap, the company behind Snapchat, is creating a separate company called Dotmo focused on AI video development. Snap employees working on this project are leaving Snap to join the new startup. This is a 'spinoff'—when a big company breaks off a division into its own independent company. Snap is doing this because AI video development is expensive and doesn't fit Snapchat's main social media business. By spinning it off, Dotmo can focus purely on AI video technology and potentially find its own investors and customers. This strategy lets Snap reduce costs while still benefiting if Dotmo becomes successful. It shows how tech companies experiment with new technologies by creating separate ventures.

tech-ai.title: Telegram ban in India sparks a rush to VPNs, rival apps
tech-ai.summary: India has banned Telegram, a messaging app with over 900 million users worldwide, allegedly over security and extremism concerns. Telegram's leadership argues India should block specific pieces of problematic content instead of banning the entire platform. The ban has caused a surge in VPN usage—VPNs (Virtual Private Networks) are tools that hide your location and encrypt your data, letting people access banned apps. This is important because it shows how governments and tech companies clash over content moderation and free speech. When governments ban platforms, people often find workarounds, but these workarounds raise privacy concerns. It also highlights how a single country's ban can affect millions of people globally and how tech companies struggle to satisfy different countries' laws.

tech-ai.title: Almost half of US singles feel negatively about AI in dating, Match says
tech-ai.summary: Match, which owns major dating apps like Tinder and Hinge, surveyed singles and found that about 47 percent feel negative about AI in dating. However, many users are actually open to AI helping with smaller things like improving their profile photos or suggesting conversation starters. This reveals the complicated feelings people have about AI—they're worried about AI choosing matches for them, but they like AI as a helpful tool. The data matters because dating apps are a huge part of how young people meet, and understanding comfort levels with AI helps companies design features people actually want. It shows that AI isn't just about making things fully automated; sometimes people want AI assistance rather than AI control.

tech-ai.title: The 11 standout startups from YC's Demo Day, according to VCs
tech-ai.summary: Y Combinator (YC), a famous startup accelerator that helps new tech companies grow, held its Spring 2026 Demo Day where startups pitch themselves to investors. Investors identified eleven standout companies, with some valued at over $175 million—meaning investors think they're worth that much money. Demo Days are basically giant showcases where hundreds of new tech companies try to impress investors and earn funding. This matters because the startups highlighted here represent where tech innovation is heading. The fact that some brand-new companies are already valued so highly shows massive investor excitement about emerging technologies. These startups often tackle problems with AI, climate, healthcare, or consumer apps, giving clues about which tech trends are trending.

tech-ai.title: Amazon employees say they're facing termination for backing data center limits
tech-ai.summary: Three Amazon software engineers testified at Seattle City Council hearings opposing data centers because of environmental and energy concerns. After the hearing, Amazon allegedly fired or threatened to fire them for this political speech. These engineers cited a city law protecting workers' right to free speech without employer retaliation. This matters because it raises questions about corporate power and employee rights. When tech companies grow huge, they gain enormous influence over local politics and their workers' lives. If Amazon actually retaliated against these engineers, it could set an important legal precedent about whether tech companies can silence employee activists. This situation highlights ongoing tensions between corporate interests and social responsibility in the tech industry.

world-news.title: US lifts naval blockade as Iran's supreme leader says Trump made deal 'out of desperation'
world-news.summary: The United States and Iran have signed a historic deal to end their ongoing war, with the US agreeing to lift its naval blockade—a military restriction preventing ships from entering Iranian waters. However, Iran's supreme leader (the country's highest authority) has publicly rejected the agreement, claiming President Trump only signed it because he was desperate. A naval blockade is an economic and military tool used to pressure countries by cutting off trade. This deal is massive for global geopolitics because the US-Iran conflict has destabilized the Middle East for years, affecting oil prices and regional stability worldwide. The disagreement between Trump and Iran's leadership suggests the deal may be fragile and could collapse if either side backs out.

world-news.title: EU won't lift key Iran sanctions until formal nuclear deal reached
world-news.summary: The European Union has announced it will not remove its economic penalties against Iran—called sanctions, which restrict trade and freeze assets—until a formal nuclear agreement is finalized. Sanctions are punishments imposed by countries or groups of countries to pressure another nation into changing its behavior. This matters because while the US and Iran just signed a war-ending deal, the broader issue of Iran's nuclear program remains unresolved. The EU's position means Iran won't get full economic relief even with the US deal, which could undermine the agreement's success. Understanding this shows how complicated international diplomacy is: multiple countries and organizations must coordinate, and disagreements can derail peace efforts.

world-news.title: What Iran and US get from deal and why both could struggle to keep it
world-news.summary: Analysts are breaking down what each side claims to have won in the US-Iran war deal and why the agreement might not hold. The US says it achieved peace and reduced Iranian military power in the region, while Iran claims it survived the conflict and maintained its influence. However, both countries face internal and external pressures that could make keeping the deal difficult. Economic challenges, political opposition at home, and disagreements with allies could all threaten the agreement's survival. This analysis is crucial because major international agreements often fail when countries prioritize short-term gains over long-term peace, and this deal's fragility could determine whether the Middle East finds stability or returns to conflict.

world-news.title: Hegseth renews Nato criticism and says US will review presence in Europe
world-news.summary: Pete Hegseth, the US Defence Secretary (the government official overseeing the military), has announced that the United States is reconsidering its military commitment to NATO, the defensive alliance of Western countries including most of Europe. NATO, formed after World War II, is supposed to guarantee that if one member is attacked, all members will defend it together. Hegseth's statement suggests the Trump administration may reduce the number of American soldiers stationed in Europe, which would weaken NATO's ability to respond to threats like Russian aggression. This is globally significant because Europe's security depends heavily on US military support, and any reduction could embolden countries like Russia to pursue aggressive actions. The move signals a major shift in American foreign policy and could reshape global power dynamics.

world-news.title: Moscow residents complain of black rain after largest Ukrainian attack hits oil refinery
world-news.summary: Ukraine launched its largest drone attack yet on Russian territory, targeting an oil refinery near Moscow with approximately 200 unmanned aircraft. Residents reported black rain—a phenomenon caused by ash and pollutants from the burning refinery falling with precipitation. This attack is significant because it demonstrates Ukraine's growing military capability to strike deep inside Russia, challenging Russia's air defenses and showing the war is far from over despite peace negotiations. The targeting of oil infrastructure affects Russia's economy and fuel supply, which could impact global energy prices since Russia is a major oil producer. This escalation suggests that even as diplomats negotiate truces, the actual fighting continues with increasingly sophisticated weapons and long-range strikes.

world-news.title: Zimbabwe bill to scrap presidential elections sparks backlash
world-news.summary: Zimbabwe's government has proposed a bill that would eliminate presidential elections, a move supporters call a 'governance reform' but opponents view as a dangerous attack on democracy. The bill would remove citizens' right to vote for their country's leader, concentrating power in fewer hands. This is globally important because Zimbabwe is already considered to have weak democratic institutions, and removing elections would be another step toward authoritarianism—a system where one person or small group holds all power without public input. International observers worry this trend is spreading across Africa and other regions, threatening democratic freedoms worldwide. The backlash shows that even in countries with limited press freedom, citizens and organizations are resisting anti-democratic moves.

world-news.title: Thirty-five killed as gunmen attack Niger's biggest airport
world-news.summary: Armed gunmen attacked Niger's largest airport, killing at least 35 people in what authorities suspect was a coordinated terrorist assault by Islamist militants. Niger has been battling a decade-long insurgency—a violent rebellion by militant groups—primarily in the Sahel region, which spans multiple African countries. This attack is globally significant because it highlights how terrorism and instability in West Africa continue to spread despite military interventions by various countries and international organizations. The airport assault on critical civilian infrastructure shows that insurgent groups are becoming bolder and more sophisticated in their attacks. This instability also affects global security, migration patterns, and international trade routes, making it a concern beyond just the region itself.

world-news.title: Bowen: US-Iran deal raises inescapable question of what the war was for
world-news.summary: BBC journalist Jeremy Bowen analyzes the newly signed US-Iran deal, questioning whether the entire conflict achieved anything meaningful given its outcome. While the human cost—thousands of deaths and massive destruction—is undeniable, Bowen argues that Iran's government has not only survived the war but emerged stronger and more influential in the Middle East. This commentary is important because it forces us to think critically about whether wars actually solve problems or simply cause suffering while leaving fundamental issues unresolved. The question matters globally because it challenges assumptions about military intervention and raises doubts about whether the enormous resources spent on warfare could be better used for diplomacy and reconstruction. It also highlights how wars often fail to achieve their stated objectives despite devastating consequences.

world-news.title: Iran war live: JD Vance delays trip to Switzerland for talks with Tehran
world-news.summary: US Vice President JD Vance postponed his planned trip to Switzerland to focus on negotiations with Iran, signaling that direct diplomatic talks with Tehran are continuing and intensifying following the recent war-ending deal. Vance's decision to prioritize Iran talks shows the Trump administration views these negotiations as urgent and critical to securing a lasting agreement. The US has reportedly agreed to a 60-day negotiating window—a defined timeframe for discussions—during which both countries will work toward a formal, comprehensive agreement on Iran's nuclear program and other disputed issues. This development is significant because it shows that despite the initial deal, substantial work remains to create a stable, long-term peace. The fact that the Vice President is personally involved demonstrates how seriously the US government is treating these talks and their potential impact on global stability.

world-news.title: US judge orders release of Palestinian rights advocate detained by ICE
world-news.summary: A US federal judge has ordered the release of a mosque leader who was arrested and held by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE, the US immigration authority) allegedly because of his public advocacy for Palestinian rights and causes. The case raises serious questions about free speech protections in the United States and whether people can be detained based on their political beliefs and speech. This is globally significant because it touches on human rights, freedom of expression, and the treatment of activists and minorities in democracies—issues that international organizations and countries monitor carefully. The judge's decision to order the man's release suggests the government lacked legal justification for the detention, potentially setting a precedent for protecting activists from politically motivated arrests. It also highlights tensions between national security concerns and civil liberties that democracies worldwide are grappling with.

finance-markets.title: Iran to get access to $6bn of frozen funds to buy US goods
finance-markets.summary: The US and Iran just reached a temporary peace deal where the Trump administration will release $6 billion in Iranian money that's been frozen in Qatar since sanctions (financial punishments) were imposed. Think of it like this: when countries disagree, they often freeze another country's money held abroad to pressure them into negotiating. Under this interim (temporary) agreement, Iran gets the money back in phases, but can only use it to buy American goods, not weapons. This matters because Iran is a major oil producer in the Middle East, so any deal involving them affects global oil prices and stability. For teenagers, this could mean cheaper gas prices eventually, since it reduces tensions in a region that supplies much of the world's oil.

finance-markets.title: Oil Heads for Deep Weekly Loss as Hormuz Flows Start to Pick Up
finance-markets.summary: Oil prices are dropping significantly this week because ships are starting to move through the Strait of Hormuz again—a crucial waterway between Iran and the Persian Gulf that carries about 20% of the world's oil. The US-Iran peace deal we mentioned earlier is allowing shipping to resume, which means more oil is flowing into global markets. When supply increases, prices typically fall because there's more product available. The Strait of Hormuz had essentially been disrupted, causing what experts call the world's biggest ever oil supply shock (sudden shortage). For teenagers, lower oil prices mean cheaper gas at the pump and potentially cheaper products since shipping costs influence prices on almost everything. This is one of the most direct ways global finance affects your daily life.

finance-markets.title: US-Iran accord opens way for Hormuz charges, industry warns
finance-markets.summary: Shipping companies are worried that Iran might use the US-Iran deal as an opportunity to start charging fees for ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, similar to how Egypt charges ships to use the Suez Canal or how Malaysia charges for the Strait of Malacca. A tariff (tax on goods or services) system like this could happen within 60 days. If Iran introduces these charges, it would increase shipping costs for goods traveling through the Middle East, which ultimately makes products more expensive for consumers worldwide. These extra fees would be passed along through supply chains—affecting everything from clothing to electronics. The deal's language might have accidentally created a loophole that lets Iran do this, which is why shipping executives are concerned and closely watching developments.

finance-markets.title: SpaceX plots $20bn bond deal after record IPO
finance-markets.summary: SpaceX, Elon Musk's rocket and artificial intelligence company, just raised a record-breaking $86 billion in its IPO (initial public offering, when a private company becomes publicly traded on the stock market). Now the company is planning to borrow an additional $20 billion through bonds—basically loans that investors provide, getting paid back with interest. Companies do this to fund expansion and projects without giving up ownership. SpaceX is one of the most valuable companies in the world right now, partly because of AI enthusiasm and its space technology. This matters financially because it shows investor confidence in space technology and AI companies, which are reshaping entire industries. For teenagers interested in tech or investing, SpaceX represents how new, innovative companies can become worth tens of billions of dollars very quickly in today's economy.

finance-markets.title: Currency Traders Pile Into Dollar Call Options After Hawkish Fed
finance-markets.summary: Currency traders and hedge funds (investment groups that bet on market movements) are making aggressive bets that the US dollar will get stronger after the Federal Reserve took a hawkish stance (aggressive position favoring higher interest rates). The Fed, which controls US monetary policy (how much money flows through the economy and at what cost), just signaled it will keep raising interest rates, making American bonds and savings accounts more attractive. When interest rates rise, the dollar strengthens because international investors want to earn those higher returns. Traders are using call options (contracts giving them the right to buy dollars at a set price) to bet big on this. For teenagers, a stronger dollar affects everything imported into the US because it makes foreign goods more expensive, potentially raising prices on clothing, electronics, and other items manufactured abroad.

finance-markets.title: Goldman Says UK's Sterling Now the Most Overvalued G10 Currency
finance-markets.summary: Goldman Sachs, one of the world's largest investment banks, just announced that the British pound (UK's currency) is now the most overvalued among the G10 currencies (the ten richest countries with the most developed economies). When a currency is overvalued, it means its price is too high compared to what the actual economy can support, so it will likely drop. The pound recovered after Brexit (when the UK left the European Union), but Goldman thinks it's risen too far too fast. When a currency weakens, British exports become cheaper and more competitive globally, but imports become more expensive for UK citizens. This matters to teenagers because currency values affect global trade, prices, and job opportunities, especially if you're interested in international business or studying abroad later.

finance-markets.title: Asian Stocks Near Record, Oil Set for Weekly Loss: Markets Wrap
finance-markets.summary: Stock markets across Asia are hitting near-record highs this week, driven largely by optimism about the Strait of Hormuz reopening and oil prices stabilizing. When the major oil chokepoint reopened due to the US-Iran deal, investors became more confident about global economic growth and lower inflation (when prices for goods rise). Lower inflation is good for stocks because it means people's money stays worth more and businesses don't face rising costs. The article describes this as "risk appetite"—traders feeling confident enough to invest in stocks rather than playing it safe. For teenagers, this shows how geopolitical events (politics between countries) directly impact stock markets and the broader economy. If you have money in a college savings fund or retirement account, you're affected by these market movements because most funds invest heavily in stocks.

finance-markets.title: Australian Beef Exports Face 55% China Tariff as Quota Reached
finance-markets.summary: Australian beef exports to China will face a massive 55% tariff (tax on imports) starting this weekend because Australia has hit China's annual import quota limit. A quota is a cap on how much of a product one country will allow from another. This tariff makes Australian beef far more expensive for Chinese buyers, which hurts Australian farmers and ranchers who depend on China as their biggest export market. When tariffs make products expensive, it damages trade relationships and forces countries to find new customers elsewhere. For teenagers, this illustrates how trade tensions between countries affect farmers and producers globally, potentially raising meat prices in some regions. It's a real-world example of how political and trade disagreements impact regular people and small businesses, not just big corporations.

finance-markets.title: East Asian Refiners Eye Fuel Sale Boost as Hormuz Reopens
finance-markets.summary: Oil refineries across East Asia (countries that process crude oil into gasoline and diesel) have been holding back on fuel exports for months, prioritizing selling locally instead. Now that the Strait of Hormuz is reopening thanks to the US-Iran deal, these refineries are preparing to dramatically increase fuel exports to global markets. They're essentially "front-running" (trying to get ahead of normal market activity) before oil flows fully normalize, hoping to sell fuel before prices potentially fall. This is a smart business strategy: sell now while prices are still elevated, then buy cheaper oil later once supply fully returns. For teenagers interested in business strategy, this shows how companies anticipate market changes and position themselves for profit. It also demonstrates how regional events can trigger global supply chain reactions within days.

finance-markets.title: NZ's Meat Shipments to US Surge, Lifting Total Exports to Monthly Record
finance-markets.summary: New Zealand's total exports just hit a second straight monthly record, primarily driven by surging meat shipments to the United States and dairy (milk) products. Farmers and producers there are benefiting from strong international demand and high commodity prices (the price of raw materials like meat and milk). This success demonstrates that countries with strong agricultural sectors are thriving in today's global economy. New Zealand positioned itself perfectly to supply the US market, and is capitalizing on that advantage. For teenagers, this shows how geography and natural resources matter in global economics—New Zealand has vast pastureland for sheep and cattle, making it a natural exporter. It's also an example of how trade relationships can create enormous economic opportunities; when one country has something another needs, it creates wealth for farmers, workers, and the entire economy supporting that industry.

science-space.title: Stages of Star Formation
science-space.summary: NASA's James Webb Space Telescope (a powerful space observatory that launched in 2021 and can see incredibly distant objects) captured stunning images of the Orion Molecular Clouds on June 5, 2026. These clouds are massive filaments—think of them as giant cosmic threads—made of cold gas and dust where stars are born. The images show every stage of star formation, from the youngest stellar embryos (baby stars just starting to form) to protoplanetary discs (swirling disks of material that will eventually become planets). This discovery matters because understanding how stars form helps us understand how our own Sun and solar system came to exist. It also reveals that planets might be forming right now in regions we can observe, making this one of the most important astronomical discoveries of the year.

science-space.title: Astronomers Solve the Mystery of Black Holes' Delayed Cosmic 'Burps'
science-space.summary: Black holes are regions in space where gravity is so strong that nothing—not even light—can escape once it crosses a boundary called the event horizon. Scientists have long puzzled over why black holes emit massive radio 'burps' (intense energy bursts) years after consuming stars, rather than immediately. A new study finally explains this mystery by revealing the chemical and physical processes happening inside and around these cosmic monsters. When a black hole consumes a star, the leftover material doesn't immediately release all its energy; instead, it takes years for pressure and temperature to build up enough to trigger these spectacular radio bursts. This breakthrough allows astronomers to predict when and where these burps will occur, giving us better tools to study black holes and understand some of the universe's most extreme physics.

science-space.title: Scientists Trace High-Energy Ghost Particle to the 'Shadow Blaster' Galaxy
science-space.summary: Scientists have made a groundbreaking discovery by tracing a high-energy ghost particle—a neutrino (a nearly invisible subatomic particle that streams through the universe)—back to its source in a distant galaxy nicknamed the 'Shadow Blaster.' If confirmed, this would be the first time researchers have directly linked a high-energy neutrino to a specific dusty star-forming galaxy. Neutrinos are incredibly hard to detect because they barely interact with normal matter, passing through Earth by the trillions every second without touching anything. This discovery is significant because it helps astronomers understand where the universe's most energetic particles come from and what cosmic events produce them. The breakthrough opens new ways to study the universe by using 'ghost particles' as messengers, complementing traditional methods like looking at visible light or radio waves.

science-space.title: James Webb Space Telescope Finds a Salty Surprise on Famous 'Pink Planet'
science-space.summary: Using the James Webb Space Telescope, astronomers have discovered that one of the coldest exoplanets ever discovered—called the 'Pink Planet' because of its unusual color—contains salt in its atmosphere. An exoplanet is any planet orbiting a star other than our Sun, and finding one this cold was already remarkable; discovering salt there makes it even more intriguing. Salt is a compound made of sodium and chlorine, the same stuff in table salt, and its presence suggests complex chemistry happening in this distant world's atmosphere. This discovery matters because salt in an exoplanet's atmosphere tells us about the planet's history and composition, potentially revealing clues about whether conditions there could support life or what geological processes shaped it. The finding demonstrates how powerful modern telescopes are at revealing hidden details about distant worlds, bringing us closer to understanding planetary diversity across the universe.

science-space.title: NASA Mission to Study Space Weather Impacts on Earth's Atmosphere
science-space.summary: NASA has selected a new mission called DAPHNE (Dynamic Atmosphere-Ionosphere Explorer) to study how space weather—dangerous radiation and particles from the Sun—affects Earth's upper atmosphere and the space environment surrounding our planet. Space weather can disrupt critical technology that modern society depends on, including GPS systems (which provide location and timing information), satellites in low Earth orbit (used for communications and weather forecasting), and even astronauts in space stations. The ionosphere is the electrically charged layer of Earth's atmosphere where these disruptions occur, and understanding how it responds to solar activity will help scientists improve prediction capabilities for dangerous space weather events. This matters to you because disruptions to GPS and satellites can affect everything from your phone's location services to weather forecasts and emergency services. DAPHNE will advance to Phase B of development, meaning NASA is committing significant resources to build this vital mission for better space weather protection.

science-space.title: NASA Testing Advanced Capabilities for Moon and Mars Rovers
science-space.summary: NASA recently tested ERNEST (Exploration Rover for Navigating Extreme Sloped Terrain), a compact four-wheeled rover prototype, in the Colorado Desert near Plaster City, California, where it successfully traveled about 16 miles with minimal intervention from engineers. This rover is revolutionary because it features advanced robotic autonomy—meaning it can make decisions and navigate difficult terrain almost entirely on its own, without constant commands from human operators thousands of miles away. Since Mars rovers often experience communication delays of up to 20 minutes each way, autonomy is essential for exploring the Red Planet safely and efficiently. ERNEST is being used to develop and test the autonomy software that will power future rovers for lunar and Martian exploration missions, making it a critical stepping stone toward human missions to these worlds. The successful test shows that NASA is making genuine progress toward creating rovers intelligent enough to explore extreme alien environments without babysitting from Earth.

science-space.title: Satellite Reveals Immense Scale of GPS Signal Tampering
science-space.summary: For the first time, an experimental satellite has mapped the massive scale of GPS jamming across Europe and the Middle East from space, revealing that signal tampering is far more widespread than previously thought. GPS jamming occurs when someone intentionally broadcasts signals that interfere with legitimate GPS transmissions, essentially blinding navigation systems. This discovery is alarming because GPS is critical infrastructure that powers everything from civilian navigation and financial transactions to military operations and emergency services like ambulances finding people. The satellite's findings show that GPS tampering is not a minor problem affecting isolated areas, but rather a serious and coordinated threat affecting entire regions simultaneously. This matters because it reveals vulnerabilities in systems millions of people rely on daily, and it highlights emerging national security concerns as various actors develop and deploy GPS jamming technology. The data will help governments and companies develop better defenses against these attacks and create backup navigation systems.

science-space.title: NASA's Lucy Reveals Wobbling, Peanut-Shaped Asteroid
science-space.summary: NASA's Lucy spacecraft, which launched in 2021 to study Jupiter's Trojan asteroids (ancient space rocks trapped in gravitational zones near the giant planet), recently flew by an asteroid called Donaldjohanson and revealed it to be a wobbling, peanut-shaped body with a complex history. The asteroid formed about 155 million years ago when fragments from a violent collision coalesced—essentially smashed together—into this unusual shape. What makes this discovery remarkable is that even small asteroids have undergone significant transformations through impacts and gravitational interactions over millions of years, making them like cosmic time capsules recording violent events in our solar system's past. Understanding asteroids like Donaldjohanson helps scientists reconstruct the solar system's history and inform strategies for planetary defense, since some asteroids could potentially threaten Earth. The Lucy mission is systematically studying these ancient objects to decode the solar system's violent history and prepare humanity for potential future asteroid threats.

science-space.title: Hubble Glimpses Merging Galaxy Clusters
science-space.summary: The NASA Hubble Space Telescope has captured an image of galaxy cluster CL0016+1609 (also called MACS J0018.5+1626), one of the brightest objects in the universe at X-ray wavelengths and one of the most extensively studied clusters by astronomers. X-rays are high-energy radiation invisible to human eyes, and observations at these wavelengths reveal that this cluster is actually two separate galaxy clusters merging together along our line of sight. A galaxy cluster is a collection of thousands or millions of galaxies bound together by gravity—imagine a cosmic city with billions upon billions of stars. This merger is significant because galaxy clusters only merge a handful of times in the universe's history, making this an exceptionally rare event to observe and study. Understanding how these cosmic giants collide and merge helps astronomers test theories about dark matter (invisible substance comprising 85% of all matter) and how the universe has evolved over billions of years, revealing fundamental physics on the largest scales.

science-space.title: NASA Awards Contract for Commercial Satellite Data Acquisition
science-space.summary: NASA has selected eight new companies and will continue working with six existing contractors to expand the range of commercial satellite data available to researchers, civil agencies, and decision-makers around the world. These commercial satellites collect high-resolution images and frequent observations of Earth's surface, clouds, and atmosphere, providing data that supplements NASA's own fleet of Earth-monitoring satellites. The partnership with private companies allows NASA to access more diverse and frequent satellite imagery without having to build and launch all the satellites itself, making data collection more efficient and cost-effective. This matters because detailed satellite data helps scientists monitor climate change, track natural disasters like hurricanes and wildfires, observe agricultural productivity, and predict environmental hazards that affect millions of people. By acquiring data from multiple commercial providers, NASA ensures that researchers worldwide have access to comprehensive Earth observations, strengthening our ability to understand and respond to planetary changes affecting your future.

energy-climate.title: Analysis: Solar overtakes gas power in Asia for first time ever
energy-climate.summary: For the first time in history, solar energy has become Asia's third-largest electricity source, surpassing natural gas power plants. This is huge because Asia includes countries like China, India, and Japan—representing nearly 60% of the world's population. Gas power plants burn fossil fuels to create electricity, releasing greenhouse gases that cause climate change. Solar power generates electricity from the sun without pollution. This shift matters because it shows renewable energy is becoming cheaper and more practical than fossil fuels, even in developing countries. As more people switch to solar, fewer coal and gas power plants will be built, helping slow climate change and creating thousands of green jobs across the continent.

energy-climate.title: Analysis: UK's EV drivers are now saving £1,100 each a year – and £3bn in total
energy-climate.summary: Electric vehicle (EV) drivers in the UK are saving approximately £1,100 per person annually compared to drivers of gas cars, totaling £3 billion in collective savings. This is significant because it proves that switching to electric cars actually saves money—something many people think is expensive. EVs cost less to operate because electricity is cheaper than gas, and they need fewer repairs since they have fewer moving parts. The UK government is considering weakening its EV targets, which means fewer gas cars would be banned and the transition to clean energy would slow down. Understanding these savings matters to teenagers because as you approach driving age, EVs will likely be your most affordable transportation option while also helping protect the planet from pollution.

energy-climate.title: IPVF, TU Delft achieve 31% efficiency for 4 cm2 perovskite-silicon tandem solar cell
energy-climate.summary: Researchers from the French research institute IPVF and Dutch university TU Delft have created a record-breaking solar cell with 31% efficiency, using perovskite-silicon tandem technology. Efficiency means how much sunlight a solar panel converts into usable electricity—higher efficiency is better. Traditional silicon solar panels are around 20% efficient, so this is a major breakthrough. Perovskite is a newer material that can be combined with silicon to capture more of the sun's energy spectrum. This matters because more efficient solar panels mean we need fewer panels to power homes and businesses, making solar energy cheaper and more accessible globally. These laboratory breakthroughs eventually become affordable commercial products that will help transition the world away from fossil fuels within the next decade.

energy-climate.title: Q&A: What do China's provincial five-year plans say about climate and energy?
energy-climate.summary: China's regional governments have all released their 15th five-year plans, which outline economic goals and environmental targets for 2026-2030. Five-year plans are detailed government blueprints that set priorities for development, investment, and policy in each region. China is the world's largest emitter of greenhouse gases, so what its provinces commit to doing matters enormously for global climate goals. These provincial plans show how China intends to balance economic growth with climate action through renewable energy expansion, efficiency improvements, and emissions reductions. This is important because China's climate policies influence global markets—as China builds more solar and wind farms, it drives down prices worldwide, making clean energy affordable everywhere. Understanding China's climate commitments helps predict whether the world can limit dangerous warming.

energy-climate.title: India may exceed 60 GW of solar cell capacity by end of Q1 2027
energy-climate.summary: India's domestic solar cell manufacturing capacity is expected to reach 60 gigawatts (GW) by early 2027, driven by government policies encouraging local production. A gigawatt is enough electricity to power roughly 750,000 homes. Localization policies mean governments incentivize companies to manufacture products domestically rather than importing them, creating local jobs and reducing costs. India is pursuing this strategy to become energy independent and reduce reliance on imported solar technology. This matters globally because India has over 1.4 billion people and growing electricity demand. As India builds massive solar manufacturing capacity, it becomes cheaper and easier for developing countries everywhere to access solar panels. However, analysts warn that rapid expansion could flood the market and reduce profit margins, potentially making some companies less stable.

energy-climate.title: Argentina advances 232 battery bids in AlmaSADI tender
energy-climate.summary: Argentina has approved 232 of 235 battery storage project proposals representing 8.3 gigawatts of capacity, far exceeding its original goal of 700 megawatts (a tender is a competitive bidding process for government contracts). Battery storage is crucial because solar and wind power are intermittent—they only generate electricity when the sun shines or wind blows. Batteries store excess energy for use when conditions aren't ideal, making renewable energy reliable. Argentina received almost 12 times more bids than expected, showing that companies worldwide are desperate to invest in battery technology, recognizing it as essential infrastructure for the clean energy future. This investor enthusiasm signals confidence that renewable energy is becoming the dominant global energy system. For teenagers, this means the careers and technologies shaping your future will likely center on battery innovation and renewable energy deployment.

energy-climate.title: Oxford PV achieves 25.6% efficiency for perovskite-silicon tandem module based on shingled design
energy-climate.summary: Oxford PV, a UK-based solar company, achieved 25.6% efficiency for a commercial-scale perovskite-silicon solar module using an innovative shingled design that reduces energy losses. Shingling means overlapping solar cells slightly like roof shingles, which minimizes wasted space and shadows that normally reduce efficiency. This breakthrough matters because it bridges the gap between laboratory research (which achieved 31% efficiency in article 15) and real-world commercial products that people can actually buy and install. The design also improves durability and overall performance of panels, which means they'll last longer and generate more energy over their lifetime. This represents the kind of practical engineering innovation that makes renewable energy cheaper and more competitive against fossil fuels, accelerating the transition to clean energy worldwide.

energy-climate.title: FRV secures 2.3GW of grid capacity for German renewable projects
energy-climate.summary: Fotowatio Renewable Ventures (FRV), a major renewable energy developer based in Spain, has secured grid capacity for 2.3 gigawatts of solar and battery storage projects in Germany. Grid capacity refers to how much new electricity the power network can safely handle and distribute. Germany is a European leader in renewable energy but faces grid congestion—too many new wind and solar projects competing for limited connection space. Securing this capacity is valuable because it means FRV can actually build these projects without waiting years for grid upgrades. This matters because Germany aims to transition entirely to renewable electricity by 2035, making every approved project essential. FRV's success shows that despite infrastructure challenges, major companies still see Europe as a profitable place to invest in clean energy, signaling confidence in the transition away from coal and gas.

energy-climate.title: Cadeler completes installation of 100 turbines at Sofia Wind Farm
energy-climate.summary: Cadeler, a Danish offshore wind installation company, has finished installing all 100 Siemens Gamesa wind turbines at RWE's Sofia wind farm in the North Sea, creating 1.4 gigawatts of clean electricity capacity. Offshore wind farms are built in oceans and are superior to onshore (land-based) farms because wind blows stronger and more consistently over water. One gigawatt powers roughly 750,000 homes, so Sofia will supply electricity to over a million people across Europe. This project demonstrates that large-scale renewable infrastructure is fully operational and reliable—wind farms don't just exist in theory anymore; they're real, completed projects generating actual electricity. The completion of major wind projects like Sofia shows investors that renewable energy is a mature, bankable industry. For your generation, this means good-paying jobs in renewable energy installation, maintenance, and engineering will be abundant throughout your careers.

energy-climate.title: Portugal plans capacity market with dedicated battery storage auction
energy-climate.summary: Portugal is developing a capacity market—a trading system where energy providers compete to supply reliable electricity—with a dedicated auction for 750 megavolt-amperes (MVA) of battery storage capacity. A capacity market ensures grid reliability by requiring providers to guarantee they can deliver power when needed, with batteries being crucial for this guarantee during peak demand hours. Portugal is already a renewable energy leader, with wind and solar providing over 60% of its electricity, but it needs storage solutions to handle periods when the sun doesn't shine and wind doesn't blow. By auctioning storage capacity, Portugal attracts investment in batteries while ensuring the grid remains stable as coal and gas plants retire. This policy approach is spreading across Europe and globally, showing governments recognize that batteries are essential infrastructure alongside renewables. Storage technologies you invest in or work with during your career will likely shape how electricity grids function worldwide.

As young sisters growing up in Las Vegas, we didn’t have the language to define our fascination with science. For Angel, it was an early obsession with questions about health and fairness: Why do some people get sick and others don’t? Why do some communities struggle more than others? Why isn’t there always a solution?  […]

But for many children, their path to success in Algebra I is formed years before they take the class. Third grade math scores can strongly […]

asia-pacific.summary:
Plans for US Vice-President J.D. Vance to travel to Switzerland for a new round of talks with Iran have been delayed, the White House said on Thursday.
In a statement, the White House said plans for the technical talks had not yet been finalised and that the US delegation remained prepared to depart at the first available opportunity.
“The logistics of these negotiations have never been simple or predictable,” the statement said.

asia-pacific.title: Drowning of Indonesian teen at tourist spot spurs calls for tighter safety rules
asia-pacific.summary:
Yusran first learned about the danger facing his teenage cousin, Elmi Febrianti, at an Indonesian tourist attraction in South Sulawesi when he got a phone call from a family member who sounded frantic.
Elmi, a 17-year-old Indonesian student, had been at a popular photo spot in Apparalang, Bulukumba Regency, and had fallen into the sea in the afternoon on June 7 while taking a selfie on the picturesque limestone cliffs there.
A friend who was with the teen at the time had called her family...

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong property sales more measured as interest rate increase looms
asia-pacific.summary:
Hong Kong homebuyers showed interest in 130 flats put on sale by developers on Thursday evening, but the pace of buying was more measured after an interest-rate increase later this year became more likely.
Agents said 30 of the 75 units put on sale at Phase 4B of La Montagne at the Southside project at Wong Chuk Hang MTR station sold on Thursday.
The two-bedroom units had saleable areas ranging from 484 to 546 sq ft, with prices starting at HK$13.4 million (US$1.7 million) after discounts.
With.

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong surgeon misidentified organs in fatal blunder, hospital probe finds
asia-pacific.summary:
A Hong Kong surgeon who operated on the wrong organ of an elderly patient had shown “confirmation bias” in identifying structures in the abdominal cavity, an investigation into the blunder has found, prompting a former lawmaker to call for his dismissal.
Tseung Kwan O Hospital on Thursday released a cause analysis report on the February 7 incident involving an 85-year-old woman with obstructive sigmoid colon cancer, who died three weeks after the operation.

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong’s local brands hold key to city’s creative future
asia-pacific.summary:
As a cultural strategist, my work takes me across the region as I explore new markets, mine local insights and connect with new consumers. Most recently I was in Seoul, a city voted for four consecutive years by millennial and Gen Z readers of a US-based travel media outlet as their favourite place to visit.
Walking the streets of cool-kid neighbourhoods such as Hannam-dong, Seochon and Seongsu-dong on my usual market visits, I noticed something. Locals and tourists alike were walking around...

asia-pacific.title: Why West doesn’t understand China, according to ex-Harvard Chinese author
asia-pacific.summary:
Nie Huihua, a professor of economics at Renmin University, discusses how an effective understanding of China’s development requires a shift from Western-centric frameworks to an integrated perspective that recognises how formal institutions, informal grass-roots mechanisms and cultural collectivism couple together to create a self-consistent and adaptive governance system.
SCMP Plus readers get early access to articles in the Open Questions series.

asia-pacific.title: South Korean police detain 12-year-olds in rare crackdown on young offenders
asia-pacific.summary:
South Korean police have taken the rare step of placing three 12-year-old boys in juvenile facilities after they allegedly stole cars and drove without licences in two separate incidents within a week, underscoring growing concern over serious offences committed by children too young to face criminal prosecution.
Police in Cheonan, a city about 90km south of Seoul, said on June 17 that the boys had been placed in juvenile protection facilities following a series of car thefts in May.
“These...

asia-pacific.title: The Chinese scientists hoping to use a space solar plant to power the world
asia-pacific.summary:
Using a few mirrors, solar panels and a microwave transmitter, Fan Guanheng and his team can send power over 100 metres (330 feet) through the air. But they are dreaming much bigger – 36,000km (23,460 miles) bigger.
The team at Xidian University in Xian, capital of northwest China’s Shaanxi province, are testing hardware that could one day be used to generate power in space and send it back to Earth.
On a sweltering June morning, the team were measuring how well light was concentrated by a...

asia-pacific.title: Chinese AI improves forecasts as Hong Kong braces for super typhoons
asia-pacific.summary:
An artificial intelligence model recently deployed at the Hong Kong Observatory and mainland China’s National Meteorological Centre can solve one of the toughest challenges in weather forecasting: predicting when a typhoon will rapidly intensify.
Li Qinglan, a professor at the Shenzhen Institutes of Advanced Technology (SIAT) who is leading the project, said the system was installed around three weeks ago and had provided “real-time updates on the progression of Typhoon Jangmi”.
Jangmi, which...

asia-pacific.title: China’s ‘soy sauce’ intestinal detox scam swindles over 100 seniors out of US$1.5 million
asia-pacific.summary:
A Chinese health centre scammed more than 100 elderly people out of a total of over 10 million yuan (US$1.5 million) by putting soy sauce in an intestinal cleaning liquid to dupe them into thinking they had toxins in their bodies.
The Beijing police have arrested more than 30 people involved on suspicion of fraud.
They cracked the case after the family of a woman in her 60s discovered she had spent 700,000 yuan (US$104,000) in the health centre.
The victim, surnamed Li, bought numerous expensive
